summarization.a: The figure shows Raman spectra of a pristine MoS2 layer compared to the MoS2 layer after exposure to Ar+ ions at different grid voltages.
summarization.b: The figure displays the Ion Energy Distribution Function (IEDF) for Ar+ at different ion energies.

data_extraction.a:
| Sample | Raman Shift (cm⁻¹) |
|---|---|
| Pristine | Peaks at 385, 405 |
| 1st-70V 2 min |  |
| 1st-50V 2 min |  |
| 1st-30V 2 min |  |
| 1st-10V 10 min | Peaks at 385, 405 |
| 1st-10V 2 min | Peaks at 385, 405 |
data_extraction.b:
| Sample | Ion Energy (eV) |
|---|---|
| 10V | Peak at 20 |
| 30V | Peak at 20 |
| 50V | Peak at 50 |
| 70V | Peak at 70 |

a.question: What were the grid voltages of this experiment for the Ar+ beam?
How did the different Ar+ beam voltages affect the MoS2 layer?
a.answer: When the MoS2 was exposed to the Ar+ ion source for 2 min with a grid voltage higher than +30 V, no Raman peaks were observed, indicating that the MoS2 was removed. However, when the MoS2 was exposed to the Ar+ ion beam using a grid voltage of +10 V for 2 and 10 min, no change was observed. This indicates that there was no change to the MoS2 material when +10 V was used to accelerate the Ar+ ion beam.

b.question: What was the flow rate of Ar+ ions in this experiment?
How does the applied grid voltage affect the ion energy distribution?
b.answer: At low voltages, the energy distribution is broad and the overall intensity is low. At higher voltages, the distribution becomes more narrow and the intensity increases.
